Device

Device is the request/response half of the library — for hardware that's reachable at any time and can answer a request as soon as it arrives.

Constructing a Device​

struct Params {
  const char *id;
  const char *name;
  const char *requestTopic;
  const char *responseTopic;
  etl::span<Query> queries;
  etl::span<Action> actions;
  ArduinoJson::Allocator &requestAllocator;
  ArduinoJson::Allocator &responseAllocator;
};

requestTopic/responseTopic are plain strings the library never interprets — they exist purely so your transport code (and anything consuming discovery()) knows where to send requests and where to expect responses. Nothing stops you from leaving them empty if your transport doesn't need named topics (e.g. a single bidirectional serial link).

requestAllocator/responseAllocator should normally be two separate StaticBufferAllocator instances — see Memory Management for why sharing one between concurrently-live documents is unsafe.

discovery()​

void discovery(char *jsonStringResponse, size_t jsonStringResponseSize);

Serializes just the device's identity — not its schema:

{
  "id": "living-room-sensor-01",
  "name": "Living Room Sensor",
  "requestTopic": "...",
  "responseTopic": "..."
}

Typically published once (e.g. on boot, or periodically) to a well-known discovery topic so whatever's consuming your devices knows this one exists and where to talk to it. Call schema() (or send a __SCHEMA__ query) to learn what it can actually do.

schema()​

void schema(const char *requestId, ArduinoJson::JsonDocument &responseDoc, char *jsonStringResponse, size_t jsonStringResponseSize);

Serializes every registered query and action, each via its own serialize():

{
  "requestId": "r3",
  "status": "OK",
  "results": {
    "queries": [
      {
        "name": "temperature",
        "results": [{ "name": "celsius", "type": "FLOAT", "required": true }]
      }
    ],
    "actions": [
      {
        "name": "setLedColor",
        "parameters": [{ "name": "hex", "type": "COLOR", "required": true }],
        "results": [{ "name": "applied", "type": "BOOL", "required": true }]
      }
    ]
  }
}

You rarely call this directly — request() calls it for you whenever the incoming query name matches the reserved schema query name (ESP_COMMANDER_SCHEMA_QUERY, "__SCHEMA__" by default).

request() — the main entry point​

void request(const char *jsonStringRequest, char *jsonStringResponse, size_t jsonStringResponseSize);

Request format​

{ "requestId": "<string>", "query": "<name>" }

or

{ "requestId": "<string>", "action": "<name>", "parameters": { ... } }

Rules enforced by validateFormat():

  • requestId must be present and a JSON string.
  • Exactly one of query / action must be present — both or neither is a format error.
  • For action, parameters must be present and a JSON object (even {} if the action takes no parameters — an action request with parameters entirely missing is rejected as a format error, not treated as "no parameters").
  • For query, parameters is ignored if present.

Response format​

Success:

{ "requestId": "<echoed>", "status": "OK", "results": { ... } }

Error:

{
  "requestId": "<echoed, or null — see below>",
  "status": "ERROR",
  "code": "<error string>"
}

Full outcome table​

ConditionResult
Input isn't valid JSONLogs INVALID_JSON, writes nothing to jsonStringResponse — see Troubleshooting
requestId missing/not-a-string, or both/neither of query/action present{"requestId": null, "status": "ERROR", "code": "INVALID_FORMAT"} — requestId is reported as null here because the library can't safely echo back a value it never validated as a string
query == "__SCHEMA__"Full schema response (see above)
query doesn't match any registered QueryQUERY_NOT_FOUND
action doesn't match any registered ActionACTION_NOT_FOUND
A declared action parameter fails validationINVALID_PARAMETERS
Your query/action handler sets errorWhatever string you set it to
A declared query result fails validationINVALID_PARAMETERS — see the inconsistency note below
A declared action result fails validationINVALID_RESULTS
Everything validates{"requestId": ..., "status": "OK", "results": {...}}

:::caution Error code inconsistency between queries and actions When a query's result fails validateResult(), the current implementation reports INVALID_PARAMETERS — the same code used for a bad incoming action parameter — rather than INVALID_RESULTS. Actions correctly report INVALID_RESULTS in the equivalent situation. If you're writing a client that branches on error codes, treat INVALID_PARAMETERS from a query response as "one of this query's results failed to validate," not as a parameter problem (queries don't take parameters at all). :::

Handler execution order (per request)​

  1. Parse JSON.
  2. Validate request format.
  3. Look up the query/action by name.
  4. (Action only) Validate every declared parameter against the incoming parameters object, in declaration order. First failure aborts with INVALID_PARAMETERS.
  5. Call your handler.
  6. If your handler set error, abort with that code — no result validation happens.
  7. Validate every declared result the handler produced, in declaration order. First failure aborts.
  8. Serialize the success response.

Next: Sleepy Devices.
